ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper offers a moderate novelty: it extends prior metric-evaluation work (e.g., 2006–2017 studies on BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE validity) by systematically comparing a large set of 21 metrics (including grammar-based measures) across three datasets and three modern end-to-end NLG systems and by releasing code/data. The claim of being first to evaluate grammar-based metrics (GBMs) at the sentence level and the construction of a combined metric (RAINBOW) provide incremental methodological novelty, but RAINBOW appears to be an ensemble of existing feature types rather than a fundamentally new evaluation paradigm. Given the strong prior literature on metric–human correlations and MT/NLG metric design (METEOR and related work), the contribution is a useful, notable extension rather than a highly original breakthrough that would surprise experts. | B(4): The paper offers notable novelty rather than a purely incremental contribution: it is (according to the summary) the first systematic evaluation of grammar-based metrics (GBMs) at the sentence level for end-to-end NLG, and it assembles a broad empirical comparison (21 metrics across three datasets and three systems) that goes beyond prior MT/NLG metric studies (e.g., BLEU/METEOR-focused work from 2005–2017). The proposed combined metric RAINBOW and the emphasis on dataset- and system-specific behavior provide a new practical perspective that would inform metric choice in deployment, and the multi-dataset/system scope plus public code improve reproducibility. These advances are substantial and clearly differentiated from earlier studies that primarily assessed word-overlap metrics, so I rate the novelty as notable but not paradigm-shifting.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a moderate novelty: it extends prior metric-evaluation work (e.g., 2006–2017 studies on BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE validity) by systematically comparing a large set of 21 metrics (including grammar-based measures) across three datasets and three modern end-to-end NLG systems and by releasing code/data. The claim of being first to evaluate grammar-based metrics (GBMs) at the sentence level and the construction of a combined metric (RAINBOW) provide incremental methodological novelty, but RAINBOW appears to be an ensemble of existing feature types rather than a fundamentally new evaluation paradigm. Given the strong prior literature on metric–human correlations and MT/NLG metric design (METEOR and related work), the contribution is a useful, notable extension rather than a highly original breakthrough that would surprise experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers notable novelty rather than a purely incremental contribution: it is (according to the summary) the first systematic evaluation of grammar-based metrics (GBMs) at the sentence level for end-to-end NLG, and it assembles a broad empirical comparison (21 metrics across three datasets and three systems) that goes beyond prior MT/NLG metric studies (e.g., BLEU/METEOR-focused work from 2005–2017). The proposed combined metric RAINBOW and the emphasis on dataset- and system-specific behavior provide a new practical perspective that would inform metric choice in deployment, and the multi-dataset/system scope plus public code improve reproducibility. These advances are substantial and clearly differentiated from earlier studies that primarily assessed word-overlap metrics, so I rate the novelty as notable but not paradigm-shifting.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and broadly supported by comprehensive experiments across three systems and three datasets, a large set of 21 metrics, correlation analyses and error/binned analyses, and the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) shows strong reported improvements. However, methodological reporting gaps remain (no hyperparameters reported) and the use of the Stanford parser for grammaticality—acknowledged by the authors—introduces a potential confound; the summary also lacks detail on statistical testing, sample sizes and robustness checks across annotator variability. These are minor but important omissions that do not fatally undermine the core experimental findings, though they limit reproducibility and full confidence in generality. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are generally well supported by extensive experiments comparing 21 automatic metrics across three systems and three datasets with human Likert ratings, and by targeted analyses (correlation breakdowns by quality bins and MR types). There are no mathematical derivations to check and the experimental design uses appropriate baselines and multiple analyses; the authors also acknowledge key limitations (e.g. parser score not intended for grammaticality). The primary technical concern is potential overfitting or lack of validation for the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) across broader data—metric performance is reported as data-/system-specific—so I mark a minor issue but judge the claims largely supported.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and broadly supported by comprehensive experiments across three systems and three datasets, a large set of 21 metrics, correlation analyses and error/binned analyses, and the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) shows strong reported improvements. However, methodological reporting gaps remain (no hyperparameters reported) and the use of the Stanford parser for grammaticality—acknowledged by the authors—introduces a potential confound; the summary also lacks detail on statistical testing, sample sizes and robustness checks across annotator variability. These are minor but important omissions that do not fatally undermine the core experimental findings, though they limit reproducibility and full confidence in generality.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally well supported by extensive experiments comparing 21 automatic metrics across three systems and three datasets with human Likert ratings, and by targeted analyses (correlation breakdowns by quality bins and MR types). There are no mathematical derivations to check and the experimental design uses appropriate baselines and multiple analyses; the authors also acknowledge key limitations (e.g. parser score not intended for grammaticality). The primary technical concern is potential overfitting or lack of validation for the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) across broader data—metric performance is reported as data-/system-specific—so I mark a minor issue but judge the claims largely supported.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions (large-scale comparison of 21 metrics, first sentence-level GBM evaluation, and the RAINBOW metric) are stated early and communicated clearly. Writing quality is reported as good, figures/tables/examples appear effective, and related work/code release are provided which aids clarity and reproducibility. A few presentation omissions remain (e.g., hyperparameter details not reported, and the known limitation that the Stanford parser is not ideal for grammaticality could be emphasized more), but overall the exposition is clear and logical. | B(4): Based on the provided summary, the paper is well organized (clear flow from motivation through experiments to conclusions) and states its main contributions early (comparing 21 metrics across datasets/systems and proposing RAINBOW). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and figures/tables/examples appear effective enough to support the extensive empirical comparisons. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., hyperparameters not reported, and the parser-based grammaticality metric caveat could be emphasized), so I judge it as very good but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions (large-scale comparison of 21 metrics, first sentence-level GBM evaluation, and the RAINBOW metric) are stated early and communicated clearly. Writing quality is reported as good, figures/tables/examples appear effective, and related work/code release are provided which aids clarity and reproducibility. A few presentation omissions remain (e.g., hyperparameter details not reported, and the known limitation that the Stanford parser is not ideal for grammaticality could be emphasized more), but overall the exposition is clear and logical.

reviewer_b.justification: Based on the provided summary, the paper is well organized (clear flow from motivation through experiments to conclusions) and states its main contributions early (comparing 21 metrics across datasets/systems and proposing RAINBOW). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and figures/tables/examples appear effective enough to support the extensive empirical comparisons. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., hyperparameters not reported, and the parser-based grammaticality metric caveat could be emphasized), so I judge it as very good but not flawless.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important, widely-relevant problem—reliability of automatic NLG evaluation—by systematically comparing 21 metrics (including grammar-based ones) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and by releasing code. The finding that common metrics are unreliable at the sentence level and that a combined metric (RAINBOW) can substantially improve sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81) is likely to change how researchers validate NLG systems and to be adopted or built upon. However, the impact is tempered by dataset- and system-specific results, some methodological limits noted (e.g., parser-based grammaticality), and missing hyperparameter details, so the contribution is strong but not fully transformative. | B(4): This paper addresses a central and practical problem—reliability of automatic metrics for sentence-level end-to-end NLG—and does so comprehensively by comparing 21 metrics (including grammar-based ones) across three systems and three datasets, with human judgments on multiple quality aspects. The finding that metrics are data- and system-specific and better at spotting low-quality outputs is important and likely to change how researchers interpret metric correlations and design evaluations. The proposed combined metric RAINBOW, with substantially higher sentence-level correlation (ρ up to 0.81) and available code, is likely to be adopted or built upon, opening directions for more robust metric aggregation and calibration. Limitations to three datasets/systems temper transformative claims, so the impact is strong but not wholly field-changing.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important, widely-relevant problem—reliability of automatic NLG evaluation—by systematically comparing 21 metrics (including grammar-based ones) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and by releasing code. The finding that common metrics are unreliable at the sentence level and that a combined metric (RAINBOW) can substantially improve sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81) is likely to change how researchers validate NLG systems and to be adopted or built upon. However, the impact is tempered by dataset- and system-specific results, some methodological limits noted (e.g., parser-based grammaticality), and missing hyperparameter details, so the contribution is strong but not fully transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a central and practical problem—reliability of automatic metrics for sentence-level end-to-end NLG—and does so comprehensively by comparing 21 metrics (including grammar-based ones) across three systems and three datasets, with human judgments on multiple quality aspects. The finding that metrics are data- and system-specific and better at spotting low-quality outputs is important and likely to change how researchers interpret metric correlations and design evaluations. The proposed combined metric RAINBOW, with substantially higher sentence-level correlation (ρ up to 0.81) and available code, is likely to be adopted or built upon, opening directions for more robust metric aggregation and calibration. Limitations to three datasets/systems temper transformative claims, so the impact is strong but not wholly field-changing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The study is broadly convincing: it evaluates a large set of 21 metrics (including novel GBMs) across three datasets and three systems with newly collected human Likert judgments, performs binning and MR-type error analyses, and releases code—addressing core gaps identified in prior work (e.g. the calls for better NLG metrics in [1–3]). However, some methodological details that a reproducer would need are missing (hyperparameters not reported), and the paper appears to lack thorough sensitivity analyses and statistical robustness checks (e.g., confidence intervals, significance testing across systems/bins, and annotator-variation analyses) and notes that the parser-based grammaticality signal is imperfect. Given the strong empirical breadth but these important reproducibility and robustness gaps, I rate it a 4. | B(4): The experimental setup is broad and appropriate for the claims: three diverse end-to-end systems, three datasets, 21 automatic metrics, and new human Likert ratings with several targeted analyses (bins, MR types) provide strong empirical support. The paper discusses limitations (dataset/system specificity, grammar metric mismatch) and compares to prior calls for new NLG metrics (e.g., work in 2017 and earlier MT/NLG metric validity studies), but it appears to lack detailed ablations or sensitivity analyses of the proposed RAINBOW combination (component contributions/hyperparameter sensitivity not reported). Related work coverage is adequate and code availability aids reproducibility, though some implementation/hyperparameter detail is missing. Overall the study is thorough and convincing with minor gaps around ablation and parameter sensitivity.

reviewer_a.justification: The study is broadly convincing: it evaluates a large set of 21 metrics (including novel GBMs) across three datasets and three systems with newly collected human Likert judgments, performs binning and MR-type error analyses, and releases code—addressing core gaps identified in prior work (e.g. the calls for better NLG metrics in [1–3]). However, some methodological details that a reproducer would need are missing (hyperparameters not reported), and the paper appears to lack thorough sensitivity analyses and statistical robustness checks (e.g., confidence intervals, significance testing across systems/bins, and annotator-variation analyses) and notes that the parser-based grammaticality signal is imperfect. Given the strong empirical breadth but these important reproducibility and robustness gaps, I rate it a 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental setup is broad and appropriate for the claims: three diverse end-to-end systems, three datasets, 21 automatic metrics, and new human Likert ratings with several targeted analyses (bins, MR types) provide strong empirical support. The paper discusses limitations (dataset/system specificity, grammar metric mismatch) and compares to prior calls for new NLG metrics (e.g., work in 2017 and earlier MT/NLG metric validity studies), but it appears to lack detailed ablations or sensitivity analyses of the proposed RAINBOW combination (component contributions/hyperparameter sensitivity not reported). Related work coverage is adequate and code availability aids reproducibility, though some implementation/hyperparameter detail is missing. Overall the study is thorough and convincing with minor gaps around ablation and parameter sensitivity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper lies squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles automatic evaluation for NLG, compares 21 metrics (including first sentence-level GBM analysis), uses three datasets and three systems, and collects human Likert ratings to validate metrics. The experimental design and diagnostics (bins, MR-type analyses), comparison across systems/datasets, and public code meet the community’s methodological expectations and audience needs. While some reporting details (e.g., hyperparameters) are missing, the study’s scope, reproducibility commitment, and clear relevance to NLG evaluation make it a strong fit for a major conference. | B(5): This work addresses a core evaluation problem in NLG that is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope: systematic, practical assessment of automatic metrics (21 metrics including GBMs) across three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and three end-to-end systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS). The methodology meets venue standards—crowd-sourced human Likert judgments for multiple dimensions, sentence-level analyses, error/bins analyses, and release of code—and the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) offers an adoptable result with strong reported gains (ρ up to 0.81). From an evaluation-and-impact perspective the framing, comparisons to prior metrics, and findings about dataset/system specificity make the paper highly relevant and likely to influence community practice, so it is a natural fit for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper lies squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles automatic evaluation for NLG, compares 21 metrics (including first sentence-level GBM analysis), uses three datasets and three systems, and collects human Likert ratings to validate metrics. The experimental design and diagnostics (bins, MR-type analyses), comparison across systems/datasets, and public code meet the community’s methodological expectations and audience needs. While some reporting details (e.g., hyperparameters) are missing, the study’s scope, reproducibility commitment, and clear relevance to NLG evaluation make it a strong fit for a major conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses a core evaluation problem in NLG that is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope: systematic, practical assessment of automatic metrics (21 metrics including GBMs) across three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and three end-to-end systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS). The methodology meets venue standards—crowd-sourced human Likert judgments for multiple dimensions, sentence-level analyses, error/bins analyses, and release of code—and the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) offers an adoptable result with strong reported gains (ρ up to 0.81). From an evaluation-and-impact perspective the framing, comparisons to prior metrics, and findings about dataset/system specificity make the paper highly relevant and likely to influence community practice, so it is a natural fit for a top-tier venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares a wide and appropriate set of baselines (three common end-to-end NLG systems across three standard datasets) and evaluates a broad suite of 21 automatic metrics (including BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE, CIDEr and novel grammar-based metrics) under the same human-evaluation protocol, supporting fair comparisons. It also performs breakdown analyses (bins, MR types) and makes code available, which strengthens reproducibility. Minor omissions remain: the summary does not report comparison to more recent embedding-based or learned metrics (e.g., BERTScore-style approaches) and hyperparameter/reporting details are limited, so while comparisons are strong they are not fully comprehensive against the latest alternatives. | B(5): The paper conducts a comprehensive, fair comparison across three E2E systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets using a broad set of 21 automatic metrics (including classic word-overlap metrics and novel grammar-based measures) and human Likert judgments, which matches or improves on the coverage in prior metric-evaluation studies (e.g., works motivating new NLG metrics and prior BLEU/METEOR comparisons). Comparisons are performed under the same data and evaluation conditions, limitations are acknowledged (e.g., parser score not ideal for grammaticality), and the paper explicitly situates itself relative to prior work on metric validity and correlation, making the analysis and the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) a well-benchmarked contribution for the community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares a wide and appropriate set of baselines (three common end-to-end NLG systems across three standard datasets) and evaluates a broad suite of 21 automatic metrics (including BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE, CIDEr and novel grammar-based metrics) under the same human-evaluation protocol, supporting fair comparisons. It also performs breakdown analyses (bins, MR types) and makes code available, which strengthens reproducibility. Minor omissions remain: the summary does not report comparison to more recent embedding-based or learned metrics (e.g., BERTScore-style approaches) and hyperparameter/reporting details are limited, so while comparisons are strong they are not fully comprehensive against the latest alternatives.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper conducts a comprehensive, fair comparison across three E2E systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets using a broad set of 21 automatic metrics (including classic word-overlap metrics and novel grammar-based measures) and human Likert judgments, which matches or improves on the coverage in prior metric-evaluation studies (e.g., works motivating new NLG metrics and prior BLEU/METEOR comparisons). Comparisons are performed under the same data and evaluation conditions, limitations are acknowledged (e.g., parser score not ideal for grammaticality), and the paper explicitly situates itself relative to prior work on metric validity and correlation, making the analysis and the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) a well-benchmarked contribution for the community.

main_claims: Automatic evaluation metrics (word-overlap and grammar-based) only weakly reflect human judgments for end-to-end data-driven NLG and are particularly unreliable at the sentence level.
This study is the first to evaluate grammar-based metrics (GBMs) for sentence-level NLG and to compare a large set (21) of automatic metrics across three datasets and three end-to-end systems.
Metric performance is data- and system-specific and automatic metrics distinguish low-quality outputs better than average/good outputs, explaining low overall correlations.

They propose an alternative combined metric, RAINBOW, which achieves substantially higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments (up to ρ = 0.81) and more stable results across systems and datasets.
_summary.methodology: Compare three end-to-end data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) on three crowd-sourced datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), collect human Likert-scale ratings for informativeness, naturalness and quality, evaluate 21 automatic metrics including word-overlap and grammar-based measures, and introduce a combined metric (RAINBOW); analyze correlations and perform error analyses (bins, MR types).
limitations: Automatic metrics correlate better for low-quality outputs but poorly for average/good outputs, making them unreliable at the sentence level.
Metric performance is dataset- and system-specific; many metrics are not significantly better than random for predicting relative human preferences.
Stanford parser score used for grammaticality is not designed for grammaticality assessment (not ideal).

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Rigorist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and experimentalist with a decade of experience designing and reproducing NLP evaluation studies, establishing benchmarks, and developing metric validation protocols.
traits: focuses on statistical validity: sample sizes, confidence intervals, significance tests, and robustness checks
expects clear experimental protocols, reproducible code/data release, and precise metric implementation details
looks for ablations, baseline comparisons, and sensitivity analyses (e.g., across binning choices, annotator variation)
notices potential confounds from dataset/system selection and values controlling for these (cross-validation, per-system analyses)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates papers by checking whether claims are fully supported by rigorous, reproducible experiments and whether alternative explanations have been ruled out.

reviewer_b.name: Evaluation & Impact Strategist
reviewer_b.background: Established NLP researcher focused on evaluation methodologies and their practical impact on NLG research and deployment, with experience organizing shared tasks and developing community metrics.
traits: assesses novelty and how contributions advance the field’s ability to evaluate NLG systems in practice
values clear comparisons to prior work and interpretable insights that influence dataset/metric choices
looks for generalizability and applicability: whether findings scale beyond chosen datasets/systems and affect research or industry practice
attends to clarity of high-level motivation, conceptual framing, and whether proposed metrics (like RAINBOW) are adoptable by the community
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges work primarily on its significance and novelty for the field and on whether the proposed methods and conclusions will change research or evaluation practices.
